facts.fact: PostgreSQL rebuilds the full materialized view result on every REFRESH — there is no built-in incremental mode, making vanilla PostgreSQL MVs a poor fit for frequently changing source tables

PostgreSQL 12 introduced automatic CTE inlining: non-recursive, side-effect-free, singly-referenced CTEs are now inlined by default, removing the optimization fence and allowing full planner optimization
JSONB tables commonly use 2x the disk space compared to fully normalized equivalents, and updating a single attribute in JSONB requires rewriting the entire document
PostgreSQL does not maintain per-value statistics inside JSONB columns; before PostgreSQL 13, the planner fell back to a fixed selectivity estimate of 0.1% for JSONB predicates
GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) indexes store one entry per lexeme (word) with compressed lists of matching positions; they excel at fast lookups but should be used wisely on write-heavy tables
PostgreSQL ts_rank() calculates relevance scores using weight multipliers: A=1.0, B=0.4, C=0.2, D=0.1 by default; ranking is I/O bound and almost impossible to avoid in practical queries
Bridge tables (junction tables) transform many-to-many relationships into multiple one-to-many relationships, preventing cardinality explosion and double-counting during aggregations
Surrogate keys avoid schema evolution coupling: if a business key changes, only the source table updates; with natural keys, every foreign key reference across the schema must update

DuckDB's USING KEY feature treats CTE intermediate results as keyed dictionaries instead of ever-growing tuplestore rows, dramatically improving memory efficiency in graph algorithms like shortest-path
Pre-aggregation for analytics can deliver 150x query performance improvement (scanning 10 million rows vs 50 billion) by pre-computing sums, averages, and counts at write time
PostgreSQL REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY requires a unique index but avoids blocking reads; however, it still performs full recomputation rather than incremental updates

Incremental View Maintenance (IVM) updates only the view data affected by changes (delta-based), reducing update cost from O(total_data) to O(change_size); pg_ivm extension provides immediate maintenance via AFTER triggers
Databricks incremental refresh for materialized views now supports inner joins, left joins, UNION ALL, and window functions, automatically maintaining MVs as new data arrives
Slowly Changing Dimension Type 4 keeps the main table clean with current values and maintains a separate append-only history table, enabling both fast current queries and complete audit trails
Bitemporal SCD Type 2 tracks both valid_time (when a fact was true) and transaction_time (when it was loaded), allowing retroactive inserts without corrupting history in regulated industries
Index scans are faster than sequential scans only when retrieving a very small fraction of rows; PostgreSQL chooses seq scan if the table is small or the result set is >5-10% of total rows
Bitmap index scans provide hybrid performance by fetching all tuple-pointers from the index, sorting them in-memory, then visiting table tuples in physical order—optimal for moderate result sets
Persisting tsvector results in a dedicated column and indexing with GIN eliminates repeated to_tsvector() calls on every search, turning full table scans into index lookups
Danske Spil used materialized views to avoid creating unnecessary tables and added complexity while accelerating end-user reporting solutions on website tracking data
A 500-million-row fact table with materialized views resulted in 85% reduction in query time, enabling business teams to consume analytical dashboards efficiently
Recursive CTEs must always include a depth guard to prevent runaway recursion and infinite loops in graph traversal queries
Recursive CTEs can implement breadth-first search (BFS) and depth-first search (DFS) directly in SQL using an anchor query (base case) and recursive term (traversal rule)

examples.title: Danske Spil's Materialized View Optimization
examples.result: Avoided creation of unnecessary tables, accelerated end-user reporting solutions without added complexity
examples.metric: Reduced query execution time, improved dashboard responsiveness

examples.title: 500M-Row Fact Table Aggregation
examples.context: Analytical dashboard on 500 million row fact table
examples.result: 85% reduction in query time after implementing materialized views
examples.metric: Query performance: 85% faster (from ~8 seconds to ~1.2 seconds estimated)

examples.title: PostgreSQL Full-Text Search Optimization
examples.context: Replacing Elasticsearch with native PostgreSQL FTS for cost reduction
examples.result: PostgreSQL 18 with GIN index on tsvector benchmarked competitively against Elasticsearch 8
examples.metric: 50x performance improvement possible with standard optimizations; suitable for small to medium applications

examples.title: E-Commerce Product Catalog with JSONB
examples.context: Storing semi-structured product metadata (colors, sizes, specs)
examples.result: Hybrid approach: product_id, name, price as columns; attributes as JSONB for flexibility
examples.metric: Reduced schema migration overhead; GIN index on attributes field enables fast filtering

examples.title: Org Chart Traversal with Recursive CTE
examples.context: Computing full org hierarchy and reporting lines
examples.result: Single recursive CTE query traverses entire tree, finds descendants at any depth
examples.metric: Depth limit of 10 (reasonable for org trees); prevents infinite recursion

examples.title: Data Warehouse with SCD Type 4
examples.company: Generic reference from data engineering literature
examples.context: Tracking customer address and account status changes over time
examples.result: Main customer table holds current values; separate history table maintains immutable audit log
examples.metric: Fast current-state queries (no version filtering); complete audit trail with zero overhead on current queries

examples.title: Financial Services Bitemporal Audit
examples.context: Regulatory requirement to distinguish when a fact was true vs. when it was recorded
examples.result: Bitemporal SCD Type 2 with valid_from/valid_to and load_from/load_to enables retroactive corrections
examples.metric: Allows correcting misdated transactions without rewriting audit history

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant that pre-chops all vegetables and preps sauces at the start of service (write time) instead of doing it order-by-order (read time). Queries are served instantly from the prep station.
analogies.why_effective: Captures the idea that CPU work is moved upstream, trading storage and maintenance complexity for read speed.

analogies.analogy: A fence that tells the planner: 'Don't look over here.' Before PostgreSQL 12, every fence was solid; now the planner can see through if it's efficient.
analogies.why_effective: Explains why CTEs sometimes prevent push-down (bad) and why MATERIALIZED keyword explicitly puts the fence back up (good for working around planner bugs).

analogies.analogy: JSONB is like storing a filing cabinet inside a single box (flexible structure, but you have to unpack the whole box to find one file). Normalized is like labeled drawers (fixed structure, but instant access to what you need).
analogies.why_effective: Intuitive metaphor for flexibility vs. query performance trade-off.

analogies.analogy: Like an inverted index in a book: instead of 'page 42 has the word pyramid,' you flip it to 'pyramid appears on pages 12, 42, 189.' Search hits the index first.
analogies.why_effective: Explains why GIN is ideal for text search but costly on writes (the index must be updated on every insert).

analogies.analogy: A student-course enrollment list: each row is a student + course pair. Without it, you'd have to store a list of courses inside the student record (cardinality explosion).
analogies.why_effective: Shows how bridge tables prevent double-counting and schema complexity.

analogies.analogy: Like a hiking trail marked with kilometer markers. You set a max depth (e.g., 'stop after 10km') to prevent hikers from walking forever.
analogies.why_effective: Emphasizes why depth limits are essential to prevent runaway recursion in graphs with cycles.

analogies.analogy: Instead of following index pointers one at a time, collect all of them, sort them by disk location, then visit the table pages in order (like grouping deliveries by route instead of scattered stops).
analogies.why_effective: Explains why bitmap scans are faster than index scans for medium-sized result sets.

analogies.analogy: Surrogate key = library book barcode (never changes, purely internal). Natural key = ISBN (has business meaning, can change if standards evolve).
analogies.why_effective: Shows why surrogate keys isolate schema from business rule changes.

analogies.analogy: Keep a 'current snapshot' on a whiteboard; write all changes to a 'change log' notebook. The whiteboard is always fast to read; the notebook is the audit trail.
analogies.why_effective: Illustrates the split between fast current queries and complete history.

analogies.analogy: Instead of counting every grain in a field (50 billion), count pre-packaged sacks (10 million). Same answer, 150x faster.
analogies.why_effective: Quantifies the performance win of pre-computing summaries.

key_numbers.context: Measured against fully normalized equivalent design
Fixed cardinality estimate for JSONB predicates (planner couldn't adapt)

key_numbers.metric: GIN index weight multipliers for ts_rank()
key_numbers.context: Default multipliers applied to word positions in tsvector ranking
Scanning 10 million aggregated rows vs 50 billion raw rows

PostgreSQL FTS with standard tuning (GIN index, persistent tsvector, etc.)

key_numbers.metric: Index scan vs seq scan breakpoint
key_numbers.context: PostgreSQL chooses seq scan if result set is >5-10% of total rows
Safe depth guard for org charts and hierarchical data

key_numbers.metric: Cardinality reduction with bridge table example
key_numbers.context: Student-course many-to-many: bridge stores actual enrollments, not Cartesian product

common_misconceptions.reality: PostgreSQL requires explicit REFRESH; vanilla implementation rebuilds the entire view, not just deltas. Manual refresh scheduling is necessary.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Leads to stale data in dashboards and mismatched expectations about refresh latency.

common_misconceptions.misconception: CTEs are always an optimization fence in modern PostgreSQL
common_misconceptions.reality: PostgreSQL 12+ automatically inlines non-recursive, singly-referenced CTEs, removing the fence. Only multi-referenced or MATERIALIZED CTEs still fence.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Engineers use CTEs as a workaround for planner issues that no longer exist, adding unnecessary code.

common_misconceptions.misconception: JSONB is faster for semi-structured data than normalized design
common_misconceptions.reality: JSONB uses 2x disk space, requires document parsing on every access, and lacks per-field statistics. Normalized design with hybrid columns is usually faster.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Over-use of JSONB leads to slow, hard-to-debug queries and bloated tables.

common_misconceptions.reality: PostgreSQL tsvector + GIN index can deliver 50x performance improvement with standard tuning; competitive with Elasticsearch for small to medium applications.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Unnecessary operational complexity and cost from running a separate search system.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Natural keys are more intuitive and should be preferred
common_misconceptions.reality: Natural keys cause schema evolution cascades: if a business key changes (e.g., customer number format), every FK reference must update. Surrogate keys localize changes.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Large refactoring projects when business rules change; coupling between tables.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Index scans are always faster than sequential scans
common_misconceptions.reality: Sequential scans are faster for result sets >5-10% of table, or for small tables. Index scans have overhead (B-tree traversal); seq scan is cache-friendly.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Engineers add indexes that slow down queries instead of speeding them up.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Recursive CTEs can handle arbitrary graph depth without safeguards
common_misconceptions.reality: Recursive CTEs without depth guards can hit infinite loops, stack overflow, or memory exhaustion on graphs with cycles. Depth limits are mandatory.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Production outages from runaway queries on cyclic graphs.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Many-to-many relationships can be stored directly in one table
common_misconceptions.reality: Storing multiple values in a single column leads to cardinality explosion and double-counting during aggregations. Bridge tables are the standard solution.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Wrong query results, inaccurate dashboards, and schema refactoring debt.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Slowly Changing Dimension Type 1 is sufficient for all historical tracking
common_misconceptions.reality: SCD Type 1 overwrites history. SCD Type 2 preserves it via separate rows; SCD Type 4 adds a dedicated history table. Choose based on audit requirements.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Inability to audit how data changed over time; compliance violations in regulated industries.

common_misconceptions.reality: Pre-aggregation is a fundamental OLAP pattern. Scanning 10 million aggregated rows vs 50 billion raw rows is 150x faster, even with modern hardware.
common_misconceptions.impact_if_believed: Missed opportunity to make analytics fast and cost-effective; overprovisioning infrastructure.
